content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Counterfactual Prompting to Mitigate Social Bias in LLM Outputs",
  "problem": "Large language models often produce outputs reflecting social biases and stereotypes, even when explicitly prompted for neutrality. Existing prompting techniques are insufficiently robust to hidden or emergent biases.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, mitigating their propagation of harmful stereotypes is critical. Prior work shows LLMs not only reflect but can amplify biases present in training data. Prompt engineering offers a direct, model-agnostic intervention point, but automated, scalable strategies for bias reduction are lacking.",
  "method": "We propose Dynamic Counterfactual Prompting (DCP), wherein each user prompt is automatically augmented with generated counterfactual prompts that swap demographic attributes (e.g., gender, race) in contextually relevant ways. The LLM generates outputs for both the original and counterfactual prompts, and a self-consistency module compares responses, enforcing attribute-invariant content by re-prompting or rejecting biased outputs. This approach leverages LLMs' ability to respond differently to subtle prompt changes and systematically encourages bias-insensitive completions.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will benchmark DCP on StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs datasets, comparing against standard and instruction-tuned prompts. Metrics include stereotype score reduction and answer consistency across demographic variants. Human evaluation will assess fluency and fairness. Ablations will test individual components of the method."
}
